games.explanation: deGrom's name still moves the line more than his profile deserves, and the market is charging a premium for the brand instead of the matchup. Eury Perez is quietly the same pitcher by the underlying numbers, missing bats at nearly an identical clip, and Miami happens to run out the lefty bats (Stowers, Conine) that have been the one soft spot in deGrom's year. The sim sees a one-run game, the board sees a coin flip dressed up as a mismatch, and you're getting plus money on the side with the 9-point edge. +106 on the better team is the whole story.

The market is treating this like a coin flip because Bibee has the better season-long ERA, which is exactly the kind of surface-level read that misses what's actually going on. Bibee gets hit harder than his reputation suggests, and the left-handed half of the White Sox order, Benintendi and Teel in particular, is built to make a command-leaning righty work for every out. Fedde isn't a star, but he doesn't need to be in a park-neutral game the sim sees as a one-run fight. Getting the better team at a pick price is the entire angle, and -102 on a 60% side is how these tickets get written.

Jax is a strikeout monster who turns into a normal pitcher the moment a left-handed bat steps in, and Kansas City happens to be built almost entirely out of left-handed bats. The platoon split on him is the whole story here, with lefties posting a .314 xwOBA against him while righties get buried, and Jensen, Caglianone and Witt are exactly the profile that exploits that gap. Cameron isn't a star but he limits barrels and keeps the Rays' lineup honest, which is all this game asks of him in a neutral park. Getting plus money on the team with the structural matchup edge is the kind of pricing error you take every time. +125 on the better side of a coin flip is the pitch.

Getting a plus price on the better team is the kind of gift the market hands out when nobody wants to back the Giants in Sacramento, and the math doesn't care about vibes. San Francisco's top of the order is built to punish a fastball-heavy righty like the A's are running out, and Devers in particular has been a different hitter against right-handed pitching with a .391 xwOBA that nobody on Oakland's staff is equipped to neutralize. Mahle isn't an ace but he's the steadier arm in this game, and the park keeps the Kurtz-led power threats honest by suppressing homers more than almost anywhere else in the league. Project this one a thousand times and the Giants come out ahead more than the market is pricing, which is the entire reason +110 exists.
